heroOverview: Sparganosis is a tissue invasion by the plerocercoid larval stage of tapeworms in the genus Spirometra, acquired when humans accidentally ingest larvae from contaminated water or undercooked intermediate hosts such as frogs or snakes. Unlike most tapeworm infections that are confined to the intestine, Spirometra larvae migrate through subcutaneous tissues, muscles, the eye, and occasionally the central nervous system, where they can cause slowly expanding masses and — in cerebral sparganosis — potentially devastating neurological damage. The infection remains rare outside endemic zones in East and Southeast Asia, Africa, and Latin America, but travel medicine clinics increasingly encounter it. Surgical removal is the definitive treatment; no reliably effective drug cure exists.

keyStats.prevalence: Fewer than 300 cases reported in the scientific literature per decade outside Asia; higher burden in China, South Korea, Japan, Vietnam, and parts of sub-Saharan Africa
keyStats.demographics: Affects all ages and sexes equally; most cases linked to consumption of raw or undercooked frog or snake meat, or drinking unfiltered water in endemic regions
keyStats.avgAge: No strong age predilection; young to middle-aged adults predominate in reported series due to dietary exposure patterns
keyStats.globalCases: Exact global prevalence unknown; estimated thousands of cases per year in hyperendemic areas of East Asia based on seroprevalence studies

definition: Sparganosis (ICD-10: B70.1) is a zoonotic helminthiasis caused by the migrating spargana — the plerocercoid larvae — of pseudophyllidean tapeworms of the genus Spirometra (formerly grouped with Diphyllobothrium). Humans are accidental dead-end hosts, infected by one of three routes: ingesting cyclops crustaceans carrying procercoid larvae in unfiltered or contaminated water; consuming raw or undercooked flesh of the second intermediate host (frogs, snakes, pigs, or small mammals); or applying poultices of raw frog or snake flesh to skin wounds or eyes, a traditional remedy in parts of Asia. Once ingested, spargana penetrate the gut wall and migrate to subcutaneous tissue, muscle, pleura, peritoneum, the eye, the spinal canal, or the brain, growing slowly for years. Proliferating sparganosis — caused by S. proliferum — is an exceptionally rare and often fatal variant in which larvae multiply asexually and spread widely through multiple organs. The infection falls under the care of infectious disease specialists, and in its cerebral form also involves neurosurgeons and neurologists.

typesClassification.description: The most common form; spargana migrate in the subcutaneous tissue producing a painful, migrating, sometimes cord-like nodule that may swell and subside repeatedly.
Larvae reach the orbit or periorbital tissues, causing periorbital oedema, pain, epiphora, and in severe cases blindness from corneal or retinal involvement.
A rare but serious presentation in which spargana penetrate the central nervous system; manifests as seizures, focal deficits, or raised intracranial pressure and can be mistaken for a brain tumour.
Larvae lodge in the abdominal or thoracic cavity, causing peritoneal or pleural masses, eosinophilic effusions, or organ compression.
An extremely rare, almost always fatal variant in which larvae bud and multiply throughout tissues; no curative treatment has been reported.

primarySymptoms: A slow-growing, mildly painful subcutaneous nodule or cord under the skin, often on the chest wall, lower limbs, or groin, that intermittently swells and migrates over weeks to months.
Localised pruritus and erythema overlying the nodule as the larva moves, resembling an insect bite or cyst.
Periorbital swelling and pain in ocular sparganosis, sometimes with the appearance of a moveable mass under the conjunctiva.
Visual disturbance including blurred vision, excessive tearing, or photophobia when the larva involves periorbital or intraocular tissues.
Seizures — focal or generalised — in cerebral sparganosis, often the first neurological presentation.
Focal neurological deficits such as limb weakness, sensory loss, or speech difficulty when brain lesions compress or destroy neural tissue.
Headache and raised intracranial pressure signs (nausea, vomiting, papilloedema) in cases of significant cerebral involvement.
Eosinophilia detected on blood count, reflecting the immune response to migrating larval antigens.
Abdominal pain or chest pain when larvae invade visceral or pleural spaces.
Fever is generally low-grade or absent unless secondary bacterial infection of a ruptured larva occurs.
earlyWarningSigns: A new, firm, slow-moving subcutaneous lump in someone with recent travel to or dietary habits in an endemic region.
Unexplained periorbital swelling without fever or sinus infection in a traveller returning from East or Southeast Asia.
Persistent blood eosinophilia above 0.5 × 10⁹/L without an obvious allergic or other parasitic cause.
A single unprovoked seizure in a young adult who has consumed raw frog or snake products.
emergencySigns: Rapidly worsening headache, vomiting, and confusion suggesting raised intracranial pressure from a growing cerebral lesion.
New-onset focal neurological deficit or status epilepticus in the context of brain sparganosis.
Sudden visual loss indicating intraocular larval involvement affecting the retina or optic nerve.
Signs of peritoneal irritation if a visceral lesion ruptures, causing an acute abdomen.

causes.cause: Drinking unfiltered water containing infected Cyclops crustaceans
causes.description: Cyclops copepods are the first intermediate host; they ingest free-swimming coracidia and develop procercoid larvae. Humans who drink unboiled pond or stream water swallow infected copepods; the procercoid then penetrates the gut wall and transforms into a migrating sparganum.

causes.cause: Ingestion of raw or undercooked second intermediate hosts
causes.description: Frogs, snakes, pigs, wild boar, or small mammals can carry plerocercoid larvae in their muscle. Consumption of these animals raw or lightly cooked — a practice in parts of East Asia, Southeast Asia, and Africa — deposits viable spargana directly into the digestive tract, from which they penetrate and migrate.

causes.cause: Application of raw frog or snake flesh as a poultice
causes.description: Traditional wound-healing or eye-soothing practices in rural parts of China, Vietnam, and sub-Saharan Africa involve placing fresh amphibian or reptile flesh on broken skin or the eye. The larva directly penetrates the exposed tissue, bypassing the gut entirely and establishing itself locally.

causes.cause: Spirometra mansoni, S. erinaceieuropaei, and related species
causes.description: Several Spirometra species produce indistinguishable human sparganosis. S. erinaceieuropaei is the predominant species in Asia; S. mansonoides occurs in the Americas; S. theileri in Africa. The specific species rarely changes clinical management but is relevant for epidemiological tracing.

Extremely rarely, S. proliferum larvae retain the capacity for asexual budding in human tissues, producing hundreds of daughter spargana throughout the body. This variant does not follow the same route of infection and is thought to involve a distinct biological switch in larval development.

riskFactors.factor: Consuming raw or undercooked frog or snake meat
riskFactors.description: The strongest dietary risk; thoroughly cooking amphibians and reptiles kills spargana. This practice is especially prevalent in rural East Asia, parts of Southeast Asia, and sub-Saharan Africa.

riskFactors.factor: Drinking unfiltered surface water in endemic areas
riskFactors.description: Pond, stream, or spring water in tropical and subtropical regions can contain infected Cyclops copepods. Boiling or filtering water eliminates this route of transmission.

riskFactors.factor: Use of traditional poultices made from raw animal tissue
riskFactors.description: Applying raw frog or snake flesh to wounds or eyes for therapeutic purposes introduces larvae directly into tissue. Avoiding this practice prevents this uncommon but direct infection route.

riskFactors.factor: Residence in or travel to East or Southeast Asia, parts of Africa, or Latin America
riskFactors.description: Spirometra species are endemic in these regions; the combination of suitable wildlife reservoir hosts and traditional dietary or healing practices drives transmission.

riskFactors.factor: Hunting or handling of wildlife without cooking precautions
riskFactors.description: Hunters who handle carcasses of infected intermediate hosts and subsequently eat undercooked meat carry elevated risk, particularly for abdominal or thoracic visceral sparganosis.

Case reports suggest that immunocompromised individuals, including those with HIV or on corticosteroids, may experience more rapid larval migration and more disseminated disease, though data are limited.

affectedDemographics: Rural residents in East and Southeast Asia (China, Korea, Japan, Vietnam, Thailand) with traditional dietary practices
Travellers and expatriates who consume local amphibian or reptile dishes in endemic regions
People in sub-Saharan Africa using traditional poultice remedies
Hunters and foragers in Latin America who handle or consume wild game without thorough cooking
Immunocompromised individuals in any endemic area, who may develop more severe disease
diagnosisOverview: Sparganosis should be suspected in anyone from or recently returned from an endemic region who presents with a slowly migrating subcutaneous nodule, unexplained eosinophilia, orbital swelling, or a ring-enhancing brain lesion. The diagnosis is most often established by surgical excision and histopathological identification of the characteristic sparganum larva — a white, ribbon-like, unsegmented organism, typically 5-20 cm long, with a wrinkled tegument. Serological testing using enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) with Spirometra antigen is available in some reference laboratories in Asia and offers a non-invasive approach; sensitivity and specificity vary by laboratory but can exceed 85-90% in well-validated assays. Imaging guides surgical planning: MRI of affected tissues shows a curvilinear or tubular enhancing track in subcutaneous or cerebral disease, a pattern highly suggestive of larval migration. CT is adequate for initial screening of cerebral lesions. In ocular cases, slit-lamp examination may reveal the worm beneath the conjunctiva. Blood eosinophilia supports the diagnosis but is neither sensitive nor specific. Cerebrospinal fluid examination in cerebral sparganosis typically shows mild pleocytosis and elevated protein; eosinophils in the CSF are a useful clue.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Definitive diagnosis; identifying the intact or fragmented sparganum larva with its characteristic ribbon-like morphology and loose parenchyma under microscopy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The mass is surgically removed under local or general anaesthesia; the extracted tissue is sent to a pathology laboratory where the larva is identified microscopically. Results take 1-3 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Non-invasive screening and confirmation when surgery is not immediately feasible; detects IgG antibodies against sparganum antigens in serum.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw is sent to a reference laboratory; reported sensitivity exceeds 85% in validated assays but availability outside Asia is limited. Results may take several days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Characterises the extent and location of lesions, especially in cerebral or orbital sparganosis, and demonstrates the tunnel-track sign of larval migration to guide surgery.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 30-60 minute MRI scan; contrast enhancement (gadolinium) is typically requested. Metal implants or pacemakers are contraindications. No radiation exposure.

diagnosticTests.test: CT scan of brain or chest/abdomen
diagnosticTests.purpose: Initial imaging for cerebral or visceral sparganosis; identifies hypodense or ring-enhancing lesions, calcifications from dead larvae, and mass effects.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A rapid scan lasting a few minutes; contrast is usually given intravenously. Radiation dose is low to moderate.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies eosinophilia, which is present in 30-50% of cases and raises suspicion for tissue-invasive helminthiasis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Routine blood draw; results available within hours. Eosinophilia above 0.5 × 10⁹/L in a traveller warrants parasitic workup.

diagnosticTests.purpose: In suspected cerebral sparganosis, identifies inflammatory markers (pleocytosis, elevated protein, CSF eosinophilia) that support the diagnosis and exclude bacterial or viral infection.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A lumbar puncture performed by a physician under sterile conditions; causes temporary back or leg discomfort. CSF results available within hours.

treatmentOverview: Surgical removal of the larva is the cornerstone of treatment for sparganosis and is curative when the intact worm is fully excised. For accessible subcutaneous, orbital, or visceral lesions, complete surgical excision is the treatment of choice, achieving cure in over 90% of cases when the larva is removed intact. Cerebral sparganosis likewise requires neurosurgical removal of accessible lesions, with significant improvement reported in 70-85% of operated patients in published case series from China and South Korea. No anthelminthic drug has demonstrated reliably curative activity against spargana in clinical practice; praziquantel, which is effective against many flatworms, has minimal larvicidal effect on Spirometra plerocercoids in standard doses. High-dose prolonged praziquantel courses (50-75 mg/kg daily for several weeks) have been tried empirically in inoperable cases, with inconsistent results, and albendazole has been used similarly without controlled evidence of efficacy. For deep cerebral lesions that are surgically inaccessible, stereotactic radiosurgery (gamma-knife) has been employed in a small number of Chinese case series and offers an alternative route of larval destruction with variable success. Anti-epileptic drugs — such as levetiracetam or carbamazepine — are given to control seizures in cerebral disease and are continued post-operatively for at least one to two years. Corticosteroids are occasionally used peri-operatively to reduce perilesional oedema. The exceedingly rare proliferating variant caused by S. proliferum carries an almost universally fatal prognosis; no curative therapy has been documented. Prevention through food and water precautions remains far more effective than any available treatment.

medicalTreatments.name: Praziquantel
medicalTreatments.description: An anthelminthic drug tried in inoperable or residual sparganosis, typically at high doses (50-75 mg/kg per day in divided doses) for prolonged courses; however, evidence of true larvicidal efficacy is limited to case reports, and it is used empirically when surgery is not feasible.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Success rate poorly defined; case reports suggest partial symptom relief in roughly 20-40% of inoperable cases, but recurrence and continued larval migration remain frequent.

medicalTreatments.name: Albendazole
medicalTreatments.description: Sometimes used alongside or instead of praziquantel in inoperable cases, based on its activity against other tissue-invasive helminths; mechanism of action against spargana is uncertain.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: No controlled trial data; anecdotal reports of benefit in fewer than 15-20% of cases, often used in combination with praziquantel.

medicalTreatments.name: Anti-epileptic drug therapy (e.g. levetiracetam, carbamazepine)
medicalTreatments.description: Used to control seizures in cerebral sparganosis; not curative but essential for safety and quality of life until definitive surgical or radiological treatment can be arranged.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Seizure control achieved in approximately 70-80% of patients on appropriate anti-epileptic regimens, consistent with other structural epilepsy causes.

medicalTreatments.name: Corticosteroids (short course)
medicalTreatments.description: Occasionally used peri-operatively or for severe inflammatory reactions around a migrating larva, particularly in ocular or cerebral cases; not a primary treatment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: No published cure rate; used to reduce oedema in approximately 30-50% of cerebral cases as an adjunct to surgery.

surgicalOptions.name: Complete surgical excision
surgicalOptions.description: The definitive and first-line treatment; the intact sparganum is dissected free under direct visualisation. Leaving any portion of the worm risks continued migration or inflammatory reaction.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Cure rate exceeds 90% when the complete larva is removed intact; partial removal can fail to resolve symptoms and risks local recurrence.

surgicalOptions.name: Neurosurgical lesion resection
surgicalOptions.description: For cerebral sparganosis, craniotomy or endoscopic resection of the lesion and surrounding granuloma is performed; intraoperative identification of the worm is critical.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Reported cure or significant improvement in approximately 70-85% of surgically accessible cerebral cases in published case series from China and Korea.

surgicalOptions.name: Stereotactic radiosurgery (Gamma Knife)
surgicalOptions.description: Used for deep or eloquent-region cerebral lesions where open surgery carries unacceptable risk; destroys the larva within its lesion.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Small case series report radiological improvement in roughly 60-75% of cases; long-term data are limited.

alternativeTreatments.name: Water filtration and boiling
alternativeTreatments.description: Boiling or filtering drinking water in endemic areas prevents ingestion of infected Cyclops copepods and is the most practical public-health preventive intervention.

alternativeTreatments.name: Thorough cooking of meat
alternativeTreatments.description: Cooking frog, snake, or other potential intermediate host meats to an internal temperature above 60°C kills spargana; this is effective but requires behaviour change in communities with raw-meat dietary traditions.

whySeeSpecialist: Sparganosis requires an infectious disease specialist for diagnosis and surgical or neurosurgical co-management. The condition is rare enough that most general practitioners will not have encountered it, and correct diagnosis often depends on serological testing available only at reference centres. Cerebral sparganosis specifically needs neurosurgical assessment to determine operability, and misdiagnosis as a primary brain tumour, tuberculoma, or cysticercosis is well documented and leads to inappropriate management.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek an infectious disease physician with experience in tropical and parasitic diseases, ideally at a centre with access to Spirometra ELISA serology. For cerebral disease, a neurosurgeon with experience in parasitic CNS lesions is essential. In countries outside East Asia, academic medical centres or national tropical disease units are the most likely to have diagnostic and surgical expertise. Ask specifically whether the centre can perform confirmatory serology and whether the pathology laboratory has experience identifying helminth larvae.
preventionStrategies: Boil or filter all drinking water from natural sources in endemic regions; do not drink directly from streams or ponds.
Cook frog, snake, or wild mammal meat thoroughly to an internal temperature above 60°C before eating.
Do not apply raw frog or snake flesh to skin wounds or eyes as a poultice or traditional remedy.
Use personal water purification when travelling to endemic areas in East Asia, Southeast Asia, Africa, or Latin America.
When hunting or handling wildlife, avoid touching mucous membranes before washing hands thoroughly.
Education programmes targeting communities with traditional raw-meat practices or use of live-animal poultices are the most effective long-term public-health strategy.
lifestyleModifications: If you live in or regularly travel to endemic regions, make boiling or filtering drinking water a daily habit.
Adopt fully cooked preparation of amphibian or reptile foods if these are part of your diet.
Seek prompt medical evaluation for any slowly growing or migrating skin lump after travel to endemic regions, rather than assuming it is a benign cyst.
After a diagnosis of cerebral sparganosis, comply with anti-epileptic drug therapy and follow-up MRI scheduling to monitor for recurrence or new lesions.

recommended: Well-cooked frog legs and snake dishes prepared to a safe internal temperature if consumed at all
Bottled or boiled water instead of unfiltered surface water while in endemic areas
A varied diet that reduces reliance on wild-caught reptiles and amphibians where alternatives are available
avoid: Raw or lightly marinated frog meat (a delicacy in parts of Asia) — spargana survive acidic marinades
Raw snake meat or bile, consumed in parts of East and Southeast Asia for presumed health benefits
Untreated pond, stream, or spring water in sparganosis-endemic regions

exerciseGuidelines: Physical activity does not influence the course of sparganosis and need not be restricted except in the recovery period after surgery. Patients with active cerebral sparganosis and uncontrolled seizures should avoid high-risk activities such as swimming unsupervised, driving, or working at heights until seizures are controlled for an appropriate period as advised by their neurologist.
dailyManagement: Take anti-epileptic medications at the same time each day if prescribed for cerebral sparganosis and do not skip doses.
Attend all follow-up imaging appointments; cerebral spargana can migrate to new sites even after apparent treatment.
Report any new skin nodules, visual symptoms, or neurological changes to your clinician promptly — these may indicate a new or relapsed lesion.
Observe food and water safety precautions rigorously if returning to an endemic area to prevent re-infection.
Keep a record of prior travel, dietary exposures, and treatments to give to any new treating clinician.
prognosis: For subcutaneous and ocular sparganosis treated with complete surgical excision, the prognosis is excellent; cure rates exceed 90%, and recurrence after complete larval removal is uncommon. Cerebral sparganosis carries a more guarded outlook: the majority of surgically accessible cases improve significantly, but neurological deficits present before surgery do not always fully resolve, and seizures may persist. Inoperable cerebral lesions managed conservatively have high rates of continued neurological deterioration. Proliferating sparganosis caused by S. proliferum has an almost universally fatal course, with no curative treatment reported in the literature. Long-term surveillance with periodic MRI is recommended for cerebral cases given the possibility of larval migration to new sites. Overall, prompt diagnosis and complete surgical removal are the strongest predictors of a favourable outcome.
recoveryTimeline: After successful surgical excision of a subcutaneous or ocular sparganum, wound healing occurs within 2-4 weeks and symptoms typically resolve within days to weeks of complete removal. Cerebral sparganosis recovery is slower: patients may require 4-8 weeks of hospitalisation for post-operative care and seizure management, with neurological rehabilitation extending over months. Anti-epileptic therapy is usually continued for at least 1-2 years post-operatively, and follow-up MRI is performed at 3, 6, and 12 months to confirm larval clearance.
complications: Permanent neurological deficits — focal weakness, speech impairment, or cognitive change — from cerebral larval migration or surgical trauma.
Refractory epilepsy in cerebral sparganosis, particularly when lesions are multiple or involve the motor cortex.
Blindness from larval involvement of the globe, retina, or optic nerve in ocular sparganosis.
Relapse or migration to new anatomical sites if excision is incomplete or if additional spargana from the original infection were present.
Anaphylactic or severe inflammatory reactions if a larva ruptures spontaneously or intraoperatively, releasing antigens.
Death in the rare proliferating variant from widespread multi-organ larval invasion.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cerebral cysticercosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cysticercosis is caused by Taenia solium larvae and produces cystic ring-enhancing lesions on CT/MRI; sparganosis produces a migrating track or irregular enhancing lesion, and confirmation requires serology specific to Spirometra.

confusedWithConditions.name: Gnathostomiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Gnathostomiasis is caused by Gnathostoma worms and also produces migrating subcutaneous lesions; differentiation requires histopathology or species-specific serology, as both follow raw freshwater fish or meat consumption.

confusedWithConditions.name: Trichinellosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Trichinellosis larvae encyst in muscle from undercooked pork or game, causing myalgia, periorbital oedema, and eosinophilia; unlike sparganosis, larvae do not form cord-like migrating subcutaneous tracks.

relatedConditions.name: Dracunculiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are tissue-invasive helminthiases endemic to tropical regions transmitted through water or intermediate hosts, presenting with subcutaneous swellings from migrating worms.

relatedConditions.name: Loiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Loiasis also produces migrating subcutaneous and ocular worm migration in humans and is an important differential for orbital swelling with eosinophilia.

relatedConditions.name: Neurocysticercosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Both can cause cerebral lesions, seizures, and raised intracranial pressure from larval parasites; imaging and serology differentiate them.

relatedConditions.name: Gnathostomiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: A closely related tissue-invasive helminthiasis also acquired from raw meat and similarly presenting with migrating subcutaneous nodules.

faqs.question: What is sparganosis and how is it caused?
faqs.answer: Sparganosis is an infection by migrating tapeworm larvae called spargana, acquired by drinking water with infected water fleas, eating raw frog or snake meat, or using raw animal flesh as a poultice.

faqs.question: Where in the world is sparganosis most common?
faqs.answer: Most cases occur in China, South Korea, Japan, Vietnam, and parts of sub-Saharan Africa, where traditional dietary practices include raw amphibian or reptile meat or use of animal poultices.

faqs.question: What does a sparganosis lump feel like?
faqs.answer: It typically feels like a firm, slightly tender, cord-like or oval nodule under the skin that may slowly shift position over days to weeks and can temporarily swell and subside.

faqs.question: Can sparganosis affect the brain?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cerebral sparganosis is rare but serious; larvae can migrate into the brain and cause seizures, weakness, or raised intracranial pressure. It is often mistaken for a brain tumour on initial imaging.

faqs.question: How is sparganosis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Most cases are confirmed by surgically removing the lump and identifying the larva under a microscope. Blood ELISA serology and MRI imaging support the diagnosis before surgery.

faqs.question: Is there a medication that cures sparganosis?
faqs.answer: No reliable drug cure exists. Praziquantel is sometimes tried but has limited effectiveness against Spirometra larvae in humans. Surgical removal is the only reliably curative treatment.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for sparganosis?
faqs.answer: Surgical excision of the intact larva is the primary treatment and is curative in over 90% of accessible cases. Cerebral lesions may require neurosurgery or stereotactic radiosurgery when in deep areas.

faqs.question: Can sparganosis affect the eyes?
faqs.answer: Yes. Ocular sparganosis causes periorbital swelling, pain, and sometimes a visible worm under the conjunctiva. In severe cases it can threaten vision and requires surgical removal.

faqs.question: How do I prevent sparganosis?
faqs.answer: Prevent sparganosis by boiling or filtering drinking water, cooking frog and snake meat thoroughly, and never applying raw amphibian or reptile flesh to wounds or eyes.

faqs.question: Does sparganosis cause a high blood eosinophil count?
faqs.answer: Blood eosinophilia is present in about 30 to 50 percent of sparganosis patients and reflects the immune response to migrating larvae, but it is not specific to this infection.

faqs.question: Can sparganosis be contracted in the United States or Europe?
faqs.answer: It is extremely rare in North America and Europe. Virtually all cases in these regions are diagnosed in travellers or immigrants from endemic areas in Asia, Africa, or Latin America.

faqs.question: Is sparganosis contagious between people?
faqs.answer: No. Sparganosis cannot spread person to person. Transmission requires ingesting larvae from infected animals or contaminated water.

faqs.question: What happens if sparganosis is left untreated?
faqs.answer: Untreated spargana continue migrating for years. Subcutaneous lesions may cause recurrent swelling; cerebral disease progresses with worsening seizures and neurological deficits; ocular disease risks blindness.

faqs.question: How long can a sparganum live in the human body?
faqs.answer: Spargana can survive in human tissues for years, and cases of active infection 20 years after presumed exposure have been documented in long-term follow-up studies.

faqs.question: What is proliferating sparganosis?
faqs.answer: Proliferating sparganosis, caused by Spirometra proliferum, is a rare and almost always fatal variant in which larvae multiply throughout the body. No curative treatment has been reported.

faqs.question: What is the difference between sparganosis and cysticercosis?
faqs.answer: Both cause parasitic brain lesions, but cysticercosis comes from Taenia solium tapeworm cysts and produces round calcifying lesions on CT, while sparganosis produces a migrating track sign on MRI and is caused by Spirometra larvae.

faqs.question: Does cooking frog legs kill sparganosis larvae?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cooking frog or snake meat to an internal temperature above 60°C kills spargana reliably. Acid marinades do not reliably kill the larvae and should not be considered safe.

faqs.question: What specialist should I see for sparganosis?
faqs.answer: An infectious disease specialist with tropical medicine expertise is the appropriate first contact. Cerebral cases additionally require a neurosurgeon, and ocular cases an ophthalmologist familiar with orbital parasites.

faqs.question: Can blood tests alone confirm sparganosis?
faqs.answer: Blood serology (ELISA) can support the diagnosis with sensitivity above 85% in validated assays, but definitive confirmation typically requires histopathological identification of the larva after surgical excision.

faqs.question: What does sparganosis look like on MRI?
faqs.answer: MRI typically shows a serpiginous or tubular enhancing track in the subcutaneous tissue or brain, sometimes described as the tunnel-track sign, reflecting the path of the migrating larva with surrounding inflammatory reaction.

metaDescription: Sparganosis is a tapeworm larva infection causing migrating skin lumps, eye swelling, or brain lesions. Learn symptoms, diagnosis and surgical treatment.
